sentences.text: I am Agnes Adachi.
My name was Agnes Mandl.
And I was born in October 26, 1918, just the end of World War I.
Budapest, Hungary.
Yes, it was a very wonderful life.
Except I did not see my parents too much, because I got a, a Fraulein, what is a governess from Austria.
So my first language was Austrian.
But I could understand, of course, Hungarian.
And I only really learned Hungarian when I went to school the first year.
But then immediately, I got a French mademoiselle in the house for 14 years and then an English professor, also.
So I grew up with those languages and had a wonderful life.
I was the only child.
But we had the good life because my parents had a very big social life of Jews and Christians.
And so I never knew difference between people.
And it was just a wonderful upbringing.
My mommy was just a happy housewife.
And my father, he had a textile store and also a factory.
And he used to go to Poland and to France to buy material to bring it back for his store.
So he was a textile magnate until, until the big collapse here in America.
Then he lost all his money.
And he had to close the store.
And instead of coming to the United States, we went to Turkey.
And what-- when, when did you go to Turkey?
I was seven year old.
And my father-- there was no radios over in Turkey yet.
So the famous Dutch radios-- who were the first in world, Philips-- they sent him there with Mommy and me.
And it was a miserable six months.
But for a child of seven, it was fantastic to meet the Turks.
And I learned Turkish terribly fast.
And I still had my little Viennese dresses on.
And because I spoke the language, I asked my parents whether I could go shopping for them, so they don't have to go.
And I went down.
And one day, while I was in Turkish, shopping vegetables, a governess with a little boy came up to me.
And she talked English.
At that time, I didn't speak English yet.
She said, you speak German?
And I said, yes.
And she said, where are your parents?
And I said, they are home.
And I'm shopping for them because I speak Turkish.
So could we come home for you-- with you?
They came home.
And they explained to my parents that in Turkey, they're still selling little girls into the harem.
[LAUGHS] And they shouldn't let me go alone because I was too beautiful a child.
And that's how we became friends.
The little boy's father was the president of Shell Corporation.
And she was British, too.
And from then on, they took care of me.
And they went everywhere with me, [LAUGHS] the governess and the little boy.
And we both could see, from my house, that [?
Charleston ?] was then starting to come.
And after six months, we went home because it was very hard.
That time--
--came our Passovers there already.
And they, they thought that the radio is a spy business.
So Papi only could go in the evening to sell it because that was the only time they could speak.
It was a hard life.
But it was most interesting for a seven-year-old child.
The people, the different people.
For instance, I was fascinated-- at 12 o'clock noon, somebody goes up on the top of the mosque and claps and sings all over every side, to east, west, north, and-- thing.
And that was fascinating for a child.
And I also saw once a police running about after an Englishman, who evidently was stealing everything.
And the whole street, everybody, was running after [LAUGHS] him.
So when you're a little child, these things stay with you.
And it, it was just very fascinating.
And we also had, interestingly enough, a Turkish Jewish family who became our very good friends.
And they lived at the Bosporus, that famous place.
And they invited us to their villa.
We don't know what happened to them after the war because we did come home.
But through them, we got their apartment in, that time, Constantinople.
Now it's Istanbul.
A completely--
--different culture.
It was difficult because Papa could not get back in the work again.
And nobody really helped.
So we were quite poor that time.
I remember I always loved onions.
And there was bread.
And there was onions.
And so I was well-fed because I had bread and onions on the top.
[LAUGHS] And I always thanked, too.
But it was wonderful.
[LAUGHTER] And little children wouldn't play with me because I was stinking.
So my mother took me to a doctor, a wonderful pediatrician.
And he said that my child can have no friends, no social life because she always stinks.
And the doctor looked at her.
And I will never forget-- and he said, madam, do you want a socialite, or do you want a healthy child?
Because that onion has every vitamin in the world in it.
So I still eat onion.
So you, you had a life that you describe as very, very comfortable and happy, in terms of your family?
I had wonderful parents and, for a while, a couple of cousins, with whom I was very friendly, especially my boy cousin.
And we always played around because the parents would sleep in the afternoon.
And he wanted to be a textile magnate.
And I wanted to-- or opposite.
I wanted to be the textile magnate.
And he wanted something else.
And now, we grow up.
And it's just the opposite.
[LAUGHS] He is the textile magnate.
He was.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a wonderful life.
I had lots and lots of friends.
And I was never asking anybody, are you Jewish?
Are you Christian?
They were just friends.
Did you have some awareness of yourself as a Jewish child?
No.
I knew I was a Jew.
But my father, he didn't believe in religion.
He was the one who ran away from his own bar mitzvah, [LAUGHS] leaving a note for his father, that it's too expensive.
And I'm going up, anyhow, as a man.
[LAUGHS] And then he came back.
And so my mother, on the other hand, at least, she put on the candles every Friday night.
And so my father knew the only word, to [NON-ENGLISH] someone.
That is to-- how do you call that in English?
To [NON-ENGLISH] someone, to, to bless, yeah, that's it.
And he would take my hand.
He says, come, let Mommy [NON-ENGLISH] us.
So every Friday night, we stood there with Mommy at the candles.
But then Christmas was the big, big deal because my father felt that all his employees should come to our house because that's not a religious thing.
It's a giving.
And that's-- see, we had the 6-foot Christmas tree.
And he would have the presents and the money for his employees there.
So I grew up with the Christmas tree.
And even today, for me at Christmas, without the little tree, it doesn't mean religion.
It's just beautiful.
So that you were practicing in two traditions at the same time?
There was acknowledgement of a Jewish tradition and a Christian tradition?
And a Christian tradition.
Because we had, as I said, lots of Christian friends.
And there were, a few of them, aristocrats.
And they would come to our house and play piano and gypsy music.
And so I just grew up.
I didn't know the difference between people.
Well, for instance, my father's choice of schooling.
We had a lot of wonderful Hebrew schools.
But our outstanding school was a Protestant school in Budapest, what was famous and the best.
And my father decided that's where he wants me to go.
And he took my hand.
And from junior high school and high school-- and he took me there.
And the  wonderful lady who was the principal, she looked at my father and said, Mr.
Mandl, why do you want your little daughter to come to our school?
We don't have no Jewish students.
We have Catholics, but we have no Jewish.
And Papa said, I don't like a child who can only see one way.
I want her to be free to think whatever she wants.
So she turned to me and said, would you like to come to my school?
And I said, I'd be delighted.
And then she told my father that every morning, they have a 10-minute prayer, and they're singing a hymn.
And I always loved singing.
And I smiled already.
And he said, would you like your daughter sitting there, because the Catholic children are not allowed?
And my father says, yes, of course.
So she looked at me again.
Would you like it?
I said, yes, because I love singing.
[LAUGHS] OK.
And I was a half-pension because, I think-- that time, Mommy was looking after sick people because she had to help my father.
And our Protestant teacher took my hand every-- twice a week.
And she said, come, child.
You have to learn about Judaism.
Because Jewish religion was the first in the world.
And the Hebrew I learned, and what Judaism is, I learned from her.
Because she gave it all.
And then there was a big love affair between us.
And it, it was a wonderful eight years, until the very last, when already there was a little noisy around it and the--
About the Jews, you know, that the Jews-- having to do jobs, and the Jews do this and that.
We always heard that.
And that was the only time ever that I was called, you dirty, dirty Jew.
[LAUGHS] I-- we were in the school.
And one of-- an officer's daughter came up to me and says, you know, you dirty Jews shouldn't be in our school.
And I was very surprised because I didn't even hear that word ever.
And next day, I was just about hanging up my coat, when I saw her coming.
And I thought, she wants to do something to me.
And I turned around and hit.
And I broke her nose.
And our wonderful principal, she didn't call us into the office.
She came into our class.
And she looked around.
And she said, I want-- everybody put up their hands, whoever hurt another person, either by word or by hand.
And everybody put up their hands, of course.
And she, she didn't finger us.
She just said, now, I understand, in this class, it happened that somebody used some ugly words, and somebody hit and broke somebody's nose.
So if that happens again, those children will be dispatched from the school.
Of course, by the time we got home, our parents knew about it and were quite upset because it never happened.
But in about a week or two, that young lady apologized for me, that she only heard that word.
And that's why she used  it.
And I said, too, I didn't wanted to hit you.
I'm sorry.
I thought [LAUGHS] you wanted to hurt me.
So we became very good friends.
I believe it's '35.
No, not believe, I know it's '35 because I was supposed to graduate.
And two weeks before graduation-- and I was one of the hostesses, too, the only Jewish kid-- I got scarlet fever.
And I couldn't go.
So I was supposed to go that summer-- because Germany was still fairly all right.
My very dear friends had a house at the island of Binz, what was a very famous place in Germany.
And they invited me.
And of course, I couldn't go because I had scarlet fever.
So my first time, again, to, to know that something is wrong in the world was in 1936.
These same people invited me to Berlin for the Olympic Games in 1936.
And that time, Hitler behaved very nicely, insofar that there was no words about hating Jews.
It's interesting.
The Jewish tennis club was still going on.
And we went out to swim on the Berlin water.
And I met a lot of young men.
And I'll tell you why I'm telling you that.
And we exchanged cards.
And they took me out in the evening.
And I was there on the Olympic Games when Jesse Owens was running.
And that was one thing what I will never forget because they were just as much against blacks as they were against the Jews.
And Goering was there, big, fat Goering.
And Jesse Owens was actually flying.
He wasn't running.
And when he came to Goering, he gave him the dirtiest look you can think.
And he just went-- [POINTS].
This was another hero because when he came back, all his life, he was teaching children, any color, any religion, all the time, what the good things are and how they should be done.
Jesse Owens was great.
And I'm proud that I was there as a young girl.
And then I went home in '36.
Nothing.
That's what I'm telling you.
Because it was the Olympic Games, there was absolutely nothing.
That's why the--
--Jewish tennis club could still go.
We were aware.
But as they didn't come after us-- and, and the only thing was that my best friend had this 13-year-old son.
And they had a governess who brought them up.
And she was also like mine.
Her name was [?
Hess-- ?] by [LAUGHS] any chance.
And just after I left, right away, they all changed, as soon as the Olympic Games was over.
And they told this governess that she has to leave their house because there is a 13-year-old boy.
And she said, I will not leave them.
If they go anywhere else, I go with them.
So thanks God, the last minute, the boy was sent to England.
And our [?
Hess ?] survived the whole war.
And after the war, of course, the family went back and helped her a lot and helped because she would not leave.
So there were a few marvelous people.
Also, her lawyer kept all her money, a Christian, every penny.
So after that, they found her here in America.
And they called her and said that I tripled your money.
And-- So--
--so there were-- but I, I didn't feel anything right then.
Yes.
Not quite.
To Hungary, at this point, in '38.
And I went, then, to-- no, it was still '37, '37.
And I started to learn Montessori at Montessori School.
And in '37, then, I was invited to Rome to learn from Mother Montessori herself.
And I spent a whole Rome-- a whole year in Rome, where, again, you didn't hear any differences.
And it was wonderful.
And it was exciting.
And then, when I came back, then I went direct-- always, we went a little bit to Switzerland.
Because we had a-- we have a very big family there.
And I went then to England.
And I have been in England for a year.
And then I came back again and spent part of '39 home.
But I went three months before September-- [LAUGHS] I was back in Geneva.
And there was the time when Franco already took over Spain.
And that's when I begin to realize what war can do.
The whole Prado-- I don't know how many of you ever been in Spain.
The Prado was brought down.
The whole walls, they took out and put it up in Geneva.
And it was absolutely magnificent because it was lit up, not like in Spain.
And eventually, the curator committed suicide because it was such a terrible place to take care of everything.
And it was just too much.
It was a young man.
And he couldn't do it.
And then there was-- we went to a-- just in August, '39, we went with four students on a so-called autostop tour.
We started out in Geneva and went all the way in-- down to France.
And we stopped in Marseilles.
And we were in a youth camp there, of course.
And it was absolutely wonderful because there were Arabs.
There were Jews.
There were Czechs.
There were every kind of young people.
And yet nobody said, you're a Jew, you're an Arab.
There was love between the Arab and Jew.
And we learned each other's songs.
And we danced each others' dancing.
And I mentioned it that we, the young people, could make peace, because we don't know what it is to hate each other.
It was wonderful.
And then we came back--
--and we-- to Geneva.
And we found out that there was some kind of a Zionist meeting.
And I never belonged to any kind-- and neither did my family.
But we went to the beach.
And we saw these people.
And that was first of September.
We were all on the beach, when suddenly, the microphone screamed that the Germans just overran Poland.
And you could have heard a pin dropping [LAUGHS] in the sand, it was so quiet.
And these poor people in their bathing suits were running to the phones.
And I was absolutely-- just stood there cold, that this can happen.
And I don't know why-- I even turned to my friend, that time, and I said, you know, maybe one day, we can help these people.
We can do something.
I never believed that in a short few years, it so happens that I can help people.
But I didn't know at that time.
So I stayed in Switzerland because my parents wanted me to stay.
And it was all right in Hungary.
There was nothing wrong.
And then in '42, my friends-- You stayed in Switzerland between '39 and '42?
And '42.
I was a mother's help in the household.
And I also went to the university to learn French.
And they were lovely people.
And they had two little daughters.
And--
Now, at this time, the war already was going into high gear, as well as the discrimination against Jews all, all over.
All over there.
But somehow, we didn't feel it so much in Switzerland.
No, we didn't know.
Only-- after this for me, it was terrible.
I said, how could one help?
And then in 1942, my friend said, go home.
Nothing going to happen in Hungary.
And you're an only child.
You should be with your parents.
So I went home.
But--
--that, yes, we knew already--
Because we heard, but we didn't believe, just, like, maybe--
No.
We just said, this is impossible.
They can't do this to people.
But when I came back in 1942, suddenly, people from Romania, from Czechoslovakia, some of them who could get away from Poland, they came to Hungary.
And we had friends all over.
So suddenly, our house was occupied by young people who came to the Music Academy of Budapest.
And then, out of the blue, somebody-- through friends of friends, two French officers came.
And we had no idea what they're doing.
We only found out after the war that they were spying and helping for the French Underground.
And--
They did.
And believe it or not, we were just as nasty as everybody else.
We just couldn't believe.
Well, they didn't like to talk about it too much.
They just said, they're coming to your house.
And they said, get out of here.
And all you can pick up, if you're lucky, is your gear, something to carry.
And that's it.
But they, they were so happy in Hungary, they figured here, nothing will happen anymore.
We wait out the war.
That's what we thought, so--
Actually, we were not involved.
Horthy didn't want it to.
But he had to send armies.
And the army went and fought with the Germans.
And we knew that, but not so much.
I had one cousin, a crazy cousin, whose parents divorced.
And so he was a  little boy when he came to our house.
And when he was 12 year old-- and we got  a little letter from him.
I am moving out.
I am living with a communist family.
And he stayed to be a communist all his life.
And just before the Germans came, there was already the Hungarian Army.
He came to us.
And he said, now, when the Soviet comes back, you've got to stay here because we will need you with all your languages.
And I said, not me, Mikey.
I am not going to stay here, not for them and not for the Nazis.
He said, well, we see about it.
So sure enough, I wasn't there anymore.
And then I was already in Sweden when my father wrote that he got disillusioned in, [LAUGHS] in Russia.
And now he wants to help him to come to Sweden.
And I said, no way that I help him.
He can go home and be a communist now with them.
I'm not going to help him.
Because then he comes to Sweden, and he will be a communist again after he feels free.
So this, this was more the, the disturbance.
What happened is that, as I said, in 1942 and '43, they still had American films playing.
And yet, you were not allowed to speak English.
They didn't like that.
Two.
We have everything plentiful because, you see--
--Hungary is an agricultural country.
And I, I couldn't be a Montessori teacher.
So I wanted to be a  hotel manager.
And Hungary had this very famous Ritz Hotel where-- what's his name?
Hilton learned there.
And the manager there was a beautiful Yugoslavian gentleman who spoke 16 languages.
And he took me in.
And I learned the business there.
And he always talked to me either in French or in English, but never in Hungarian.
[LAUGHS] But he spoke that, too.
So I, I did that.
And I taught English.
Because at that time, I spoke the King's English, believe it or not.
And I left London and went to Paris.
And I met some English people there.
And they couldn't believe it that I was not British-- a British lassie.
Sorry, but since then, [LAUGHS] I lost it completely.
In any case, I taught English to, to little-- Anette Lantos now.
She is the congressman's wife.
And I was working in the hotel.
Now came-- and I had a big short-wave radio.
And we usually said-- you were not allowed to listen to the BBC.
That was already forbidden, although the, the Hungarian Arrow Cross wasn't there yet.
But we did listen.
And we got more and more frightened that-- what's happening because we could hear.
And we could also see that they don't help.
The British don't help.
The Americans don't help.
So what's going to be?
And we became a little bit restless.
But there was no way to get out.
So we just sat there in our apartment, still-- and hoping that we'll be OK.
And constantly, the guests came.
These two French officers came to the house.
And--
They stayed in our house.
But then we also put them in other friend's house.
And my friends from Berlin finally came-- finally came to Hungary.
And we got them an apartment on the Buda side of Pest.
And as my friend never put on Friday-- she was Orthodox Jew.
That time, there were no lights what come up by themselves.
